php_intro.title: PHP Overview
php_intro.summary: A pragmatic server-side language powering many web apps — from legacy to modern frameworks.
php_intro.details:
Learn more — deep dive and practical checklist:

Overview & context
- PHP is optimized for HTTP request/response workflows but also runs CLI and long-running processes. Understand the server SAPI (apache mod_php vs php-fpm vs cli) and how environment differs across them.

Local dev & tooling
- Use Composer for dependency management and autoloading. Keep composer.lock committed; use composer install --no-dev --optimize-autoloader when deploying.
- Use Docker for consistent development environments: prefer multi-stage builds to produce small runtime images.
- Use static analysis (PHPStan/Psalm) and linters (PHPCS) early to catch errors before runtime.

Performance & ops
- Enable OPcache and tune opcache.memory_consumption and opcache.validate_timestamps according to deploy cadence.
- Run PHP-FPM behind Nginx for production; tune pm.* settings to match memory and expected concurrency.

Security basics
- Disable display_errors in production; use error_log and centralized error tracking (Sentry).
- Regularly scan dependencies (composer audit) and subscribe to security advisories for your packages.

Learning path
- Hands-on: build a small app (routing, DI, DB, tests) then containerize it, add CI with static analysis, and deploy behind php-fpm+nginx. Gradually add background workers and observability.
php_intro.cheat:
// Quick commands
php -v                      # check PHP version
php -S localhost:8000 -t public   # built-in server for local dev
composer install             # install deps (use composer.lock)
composer dump-autoload -o    # optimize autoload for production
php -d memory_limit=512M script.php  # run script with custom ini

// php.ini highlights
opcache.enable=1
opcache.memory_consumption=128
error_reporting=E_ALL
display_errors=0   # off in prod

// local docker dev
docker run --rm -v $(pwd):/app -w /app php:8.1-cli php script.php

examples.code:
<?php
// simple script
function greet(string $name): string { return "Hello, $name"; }
echo greet('PHP');
?>

interview.a: A server-side scripting language primarily used for web development.

interview.q: What versions of PHP are commonly used?
interview.a: Modern projects use PHP 7.4, 8.0, 8.1+; each brings performance and language features.

interview.q: What is the PHP request lifecycle?
interview.a: Server receives request, PHP executes script, generates response, then returns it to the server.
Composer is PHP's dependency manager and autoloader tool.
PHP Standard Recommendations — interoperability standards for PHP libraries.

interview.q: When to use frameworks like Laravel or Symfony?
interview.a: For larger apps to gain structure, tooling and community conventions.

interview.q: What is the built-in PHP server for?
interview.a: A lightweight server for local development: php -S localhost:8000.
A bytecode cache improving performance by caching compiled scripts.

interview.q: How to run PHP from CLI?
interview.a: Use php script.php or run interactive mode with php -a.
Composer generates autoloaders mapping namespaces to file paths per PSR-4.

interview.q: What are common uses of PHP today?
interview.a: Web apps, APIs, CLI tools, background workers and server-side rendering.

interview.q: What is the difference between PHP and JavaScript on the server?
interview.a: PHP is synchronous by default and designed for HTTP; Node.js is event-driven and single-threaded.
Metadata annotations introduced to the language (PHP 8+) used for configs and DI.
Just-In-Time compilation available in newer PHP versions to improve CPU-bound workloads.
To ensure deterministic dependency versions across environments.

interview.q: What is semantic versioning in PHP packages?
interview.a: MAJOR.MINOR.PATCH; breaking changes bump MAJOR.
Keep PHP updated, disable unused extensions, and follow secure php.ini practices.

interview.q: What is the role of php-fpm?
interview.a: FastCGI Process Manager to run PHP worker processes behind a webserver like Nginx.
Use Xdebug or commercial profilers like Blackfire/Tideways.

interview.q: What is a typical upgrade path for legacy PHP apps?
interview.a: Incrementally update to newer PHP versions, add tests, and adopt tooling like static analyzers.

php_syntax.title: Syntax & Types
php_syntax.summary: Variables, arrays, scalar type hints, union types, null coalescing and strict mode.
php_syntax.details:
Learn more — language features, idioms and migration notes:

Types & ergonomics
- Prefer declare(strict_types=1) across codebases that aim for consistent behavior. Use union types, nullable types (?Type), and typed properties to encode invariants.
- Use return types and argument types for public APIs; complement with PHPDoc where generics or more detail are needed.

Modern syntax
- Use arrow functions (fn) for short callbacks and use match for exhaustive branch handling (no fallthrough).
- Nullsafe operator (?->) reduces boilerplate around null checks; combine with ?? for safe defaults.

Data handling
- Prefer associative arrays for simple structures, but use Value Objects and DTOs for domain models to get predictable behavior and easier refactors.

Migration notes
- When upgrading PHP major versions, run static analyzers, address deprecations, and add tests before enabling new strict features.

Practical tips
- Use generators (yield) to stream large datasets, and prefer SplFixedArray or object pools in memory-sensitive loops.
- Format code with phpcs or friendsofphp/php-cs-fixer to maintain consistency across teams.

examples.code:
<?php
declare(strict_types=1);
function add(int $a, int $b): int { return $a + $b; }
echo add(2,3);
?>

<?php
// nullsafe and match
$user = null;
echo $user?->name ?? 'guest';
$status = match(404){ 200 => 'ok', 404 => 'not found', default => 'unknown' };

interview.a: Enforces strict scalar type checking within the file.
A type declaration allowing multiple types, e.g., int|float.
Returns left operand if set and not null, otherwise the right operand.
Safely accesses properties/methods on possibly null objects.
A class property declared with a type (e.g., private string $name;).
A concise fn(...) => expr; syntax introduced in PHP 7.4.
A safer, expression-based alternative to switch introduced in PHP 8.
Use function f(): Type { ... } to enforce return type (optionally strict).
Implicit conversion of types; strict_types disables coercion for declared scalars.
Use ?Type or Type|null to allow null.

interview.q: What is the difference between arrays and Traversable?
interview.a: Arrays are language primitives; Traversable is an interface for iterable objects.

interview.q: How to destructure arrays in PHP?
interview.a: Use list() or [] syntax to assign variables from arrays.
Functions accepting variable numbers of args via ...$args.

interview.q: How to use type hints for callable?
interview.a: Declare callable type or use specific function signatures in docblocks.
Three-way comparison operator returning -1,0,1 for comparisons.
Use const NAME = value; inside classes or define('NAME', value) globally.
String syntaxes for multi-line strings; nowdoc is literal (no parsing).

interview.q: How to handle errors in modern PHP?
interview.a: Prefer Exceptions and typed error handling; use Throwable for broad catches.
Attributes are native metadata syntax; docblocks are comments parsed by tools.

interview.q: When to use strict typing selectively?
interview.a: Use strict in public APIs to catch errors early and rely on contracts.

php_cli.title: CLI & Scripts
php_cli.summary: Using PHP from the command-line for scripts, cronjobs, and workers.
php_cli.details:
Learn more — building robust CLI tools and workers:

CLI app patterns
- Use symfony/console for structured commands (auto-help, subcommands, options/arguments, interactive prompts). Keep business logic outside command classes for testability.

Long-running workers
- Use pcntl_signal to handle SIGTERM/SIGINT for graceful shutdown. Periodically call gc_collect_cycles(), monitor memory and restart processes before memory grows unbounded.
- Use supervisors (systemd, supervisord, docker restart policies) to maintain worker processes and to perform rolling restarts for upgrades.

Configuration & env
- Separate CLI and web configuration; prefer environment variables and config objects injected via DI to avoid environment coupling.

Testing & debugging
- Unit-test business logic and use integration tests for full CLI flows. Use verbose logs to inspect worker behavior and structured logs (JSON) for aggregation.

examples.code:
<?php
// greet.php
$name = $argv[1] ?? 'world'; echo "Hello, $name\n";
// run: php greet.php Alice

<?php
// worker sketch
while(true){ $job = fetchJob(); process($job); sleep(1); }

interview.a: For scripts, cronjobs, workers, maintenance tasks and local tooling.

interview.q: How to pass arguments to a PHP script?
interview.a: Use $argv array or getopt() for named options.
Starts the built-in development web server.

interview.q: How to write a long-running worker safely?
interview.a: Manage memory, handle signals, and periodically restart or use supervisors.
A library to build robust CLI tools with commands and input handling.
Use queuing systems (Redis, RabbitMQ) and worker processes.

interview.q: How to handle signals in CLI PHP?
interview.a: Use pcntl_signal to catch signals and gracefully shutdown.
Running a process detached from terminal, often managed by systemd or supervisord.
Isolate logic into services and write unit tests; test CLI integration with process wrappers.
Use fopen('php://stdin','r') or stream_select for interactive input.

interview.q: How to output progress in CLI?
interview.a: Use symfony/console ProgressBar or write to stdout with carriage returns.

interview.q: How to limit memory in CLI?
interview.a: Use ini_set('memory_limit','512M') or run with -d memory_limit=... option.

interview.q: What is Composer global vs project-level install?
interview.a: Global installs tools system-wide; project installs are per-project in vendor/bin.

interview.q: How to run PHP unit tests from CLI?
interview.a: Use vendor/bin/phpunit or phpunit phar.

interview.q: How to schedule jobs in PHP?
interview.a: Use cron or a scheduler library to invoke CLI scripts at intervals.

interview.q: How to log from a CLI script?
interview.a: Write to files, syslog, or structured logging outputs.

interview.q: What are common pitfalls with CLI vs web SAPI?
interview.a: No superglobals like $_SERVER request-specific, different environment variables and timeouts.

interview.q: How to handle configuration for CLI tools?
interview.a: Use env files, config objects, or DI to inject settings.
Restrict execution permissions and avoid executing untrusted inputs.

interview.q: How to restart workers without downtime?
interview.a: Use rolling restarts and supervisors to keep replacement workers running.

php_composer.title: Composer & Autoloading (PSR-4)
php_composer.details:
Learn more — managing dependencies and package hygiene:

Composer best practices
- Keep composer.lock in VCS for apps; for libraries, consumers control resolution so release with tagged versions.
- Use PSR-4 autoloading and keep a single top-level namespace per package to avoid collisions.

Dependency strategy
- Prefer stable, minimal dependencies. Use require-dev for dev-only tools (phpunit, phpstan).
- Use Composer scripts to automate linting, code style fixes, tests and build steps during CI pipelines.

Performance & CI
- Cache Composer's cache directory and vendor folder in CI; use composer install --prefer-dist --no-interaction --no-progress --no-suggest in CI.

Security
- Run composer audit and integrate automated dependency update bots (Dependabot) with PRs and test gates to validate upgrades.

examples.code:
<?php
require __DIR__ . '/vendor/autoload.php'; $u = new \App\User();

interview.a: Defines project metadata, dependencies, autoloading and scripts.
Pins exact dependency versions installed for reproducible builds.
Maps namespaces to directory paths for autoloading classes.
composer require vendor/package and commit composer.lock.
Use composer update selectively and run tests; prefer dependabot for PRs.
Hooks allowing commands to run on events like post-install-cmd.

interview.q: How to create a library package?
interview.a: Publish composer.json with proper autoload and version tags.

interview.q: How to optimize autoloader for production?
interview.a: Use composer install --optimize-autoloader --no-dev.
Allows updates that do not change the major version (1.x.x).

interview.q: What is platform requirement in composer?
interview.a: Defines PHP or extension versions required, e.g., php: ^8.0.
Use private repositories or Satis/Packagist private endpoints.
Use the files autoload section to include procedural files.
require-dev installs packages only for development/testing, not production.

interview.q: What is composer global require used for?
interview.a: Installing CLI tools available system-wide.

interview.q: How to speed up Composer on CI?
interview.a: Cache vendor and composer cache between runs.
Add autoload:{"psr-4":{"App\\":"src/"}} to composer.json.
Ensure unique namespaces and correct PSR-4 mapping to avoid collisions.
composer check-platform-reqs validates PHP and extension requirements.

interview.q: What is plugin capability in Composer?
interview.a: Composer supports plugins that hook into install/update lifecycle.

interview.q: How to sign packages or verify integrity?
interview.a: Use lock files, commit them, and verify checksums via Composer's metadata.

php_namespaces.title: Namespaces & Project Layout
php_namespaces.summary: Organize code with namespaces, PSR project layout, and separation of concerns.
php_namespaces.details:
Learn more — organizing code and maintaining public APIs:

Project layout
- Adopt consistent layout: src/ for code, tests/ for tests, public/ for web root. Map namespaces to paths via PSR-4 in composer.json.

Namespace hygiene
- Limit namespace depth to keep FQCNs readable. Reserve root-level namespaces for public APIs and packages to avoid collisions.

Refactoring & versioning
- Treat namespaces as part of your public contract. When renaming, provide adapters and plan breaking releases with migration docs.

Tooling
- Use IDE refactors (PhpStorm) and composer dump-autoload -o for optimized maps in production.

examples.code:
<?php
namespace App\Controller;
class HomeController { public function index(){ return 'ok'; } }

interview.a: Avoid name collisions and organize code logically.
Use namespace App\Controller; at the top of a PHP file.
Code without a namespace resides in the global namespace.
Use use Vendor\Package\ClassName; to alias or import.
Maps namespaces to filesystem directories for autoloaders.
Common layout: src/, tests/, public/, config/ and vendor/.
Yes, like App\Controller\Api; they map to deeper directory structures.
use Some\Long\Namespace\Class as ShortName;

interview.q: How to handle class name collisions?
interview.a: Use namespaces or aliasing via as keyword.
Initial segment of namespace used in PSR-4 mapping.

interview.q: How to reference global functions from namespace?
interview.a: Prefix with \ to reference global functions or import them.
Can add complexity; prefer flat meaningful boundaries.

interview.q: How do namespaces affect autoloading performance?
interview.a: PSR-4 autoloading is efficient; avoid excessive filesystem lookups.
Namespace beginning with vendor or package name to avoid collisions.

interview.q: How to organize controllers vs services?
interview.a: Keep controllers thin and place business logic in services under different namespaces.

interview.q: How to map multiple namespaces to one directory?
interview.a: PSR-4 allows multiple prefixes mapping to the same path.
Composer autoload enables tests to instantiate classes by their FQCN.

interview.q: How to expose public API namespaces?
interview.a: Document and keep backward-compatible public namespaces stable.
Use automated refactors, update composer.json and release as breaking change.

interview.q: How to handle legacy global functions with namespaces?
interview.a: Wrap legacy code in adapters inside a namespace to gradually modernize.

php_oop.title: Objects & OOP
php_oop.summary: Classes, typed properties, constructor promotion, DI and testable design.
php_oop.details:
Learn more — designing maintainable object-oriented systems in PHP:

Design principles
- Apply SOLID: small single-purpose classes, explicit interfaces, and dependency inversion for testability.
- Favor composition over inheritance; use traits sparingly for small cross-cutting concerns only.

DI & factories
- Use constructor injection for required dependencies; use factories for complex construction and DI containers (Symfony DI, PHP-DI) for large apps.

Immutable patterns
- Use value objects and immutable DTOs for domain data. Use readonly properties where appropriate (PHP 8.1+) to enforce immutability.

Testing & contracts
- Program to interfaces: write tests against interfaces and provide fakes/mocks. Document invariants and exception behavior in PHPDoc.

examples.code:
<?php
class User { private string $name; public function __construct(string $n){ $this->name=$n; } public function getName(): string { return $this->name; } }
?>

<?php
// constructor promotion (PHP 8)
class Point { public function __construct(private float $x, private float $y){} public function x(){return $this->x;} }

interview.a: Declaring and initializing properties in the constructor signature (PHP 8).
Properties declared with types, e.g., private int $id;
Providing dependencies to classes via constructor or setter injection.
Five design principles to make code maintainable and testable.
Hiding internal state and exposing a defined API via methods.
Using a common interface to interact with different implementations.
Inheritance extends behavior; composition combines objects to reuse behavior.
To prevent extension for stability and API safety.
__construct, __get, __set, __toString and others for special behaviors.
A class that cannot be instantiated and may define abstract methods.
A contract defining public methods a class must implement.

interview.q: How to make code testable with OOP?
interview.a: Design small classes, inject dependencies, and program to interfaces.
An immutable object representing a value with equality by state.
Split responsibilities and apply single responsibility principle.
Harder to mock and can introduce global state.
Use typed signatures and PHPDoc for complexity and examples.
Subtype must be replaceable for its base type without altering behavior.
Use factories or DI containers to centralize creation and configuration.
Returning $this to allow fluent API calls.
When you need flexible behavior reuse and to avoid rigid inheritance.

php_traits_interfaces.title: Traits, Interfaces & Dependency Injection
php_traits_interfaces.summary: Reuse via traits, interfaces for contracts and DI for decoupling.
php_traits_interfaces.details:
Learn more — composition, contracts and testability:

Traits
- Use traits for small, reusable method bundles (logging, simple helpers); avoid traits that mutate or contain stateful behavior that breaks encapsulation.
- Resolve conflicts explicitly with insteadof/as and document reasoning.

Interfaces & DI
- Keep interfaces small and focused to simplify mocks and alternative implementations. Use interface segregation to avoid fat contracts.
- Use interface-based factories to create complex object graphs and keep wiring in a composition root.

Testing
- Use PHPUnit/Mockery/Prophecy to create interface-based mocks; prefer integration tests for verifying real wiring once.

examples.code:
<?php
trait LoggerTrait { protected function log($m){ echo $m; } }
interface Mailer { public function send(string $to, string $body); }
class SmtpMailer implements Mailer { public function send(string $to, string $body){} }

interview.a: A code reuse mechanism to include methods across classes.
For horizontal code reuse when inheritance is unsuitable.
A contract defining public methods without implementation.

interview.q: How do traits differ from interfaces?
interview.a: Traits provide method implementations; interfaces declare signatures.
Pattern to supply dependencies from outside, improving testability.

interview.q: What is constructor vs setter injection?
interview.a: Constructor injection provides dependencies at creation; setter injection allows later configuration.
A global registry hiding dependencies and reducing testability.

interview.q: How to resolve trait method conflicts?
interview.a: Use insteadof and as to resolve and alias methods.

interview.q: How to design interfaces for testing?
interview.a: Keep them small and focused to ease mocking.
Prefer multiple specific interfaces over one large interface.
Use testing frameworks like PHPUnit to create mocks based on interfaces.

interview.q: When to use abstract classes over interfaces?
interview.a: When you need shared implementation as well as contracts.
Resolve and instantiate services and their dependencies.
Limit traits to small, well-scoped behavior to keep clarity.
Prefer composing behavior from small objects rather than deep inheritance.

interview.q: How to design extension points with interfaces?
interview.a: Expose interfaces for replaceable components and keep implementations private.
Avoid breaking changes; add new interfaces instead of modifying existing ones.

interview.q: How to handle optional dependencies in DI?
interview.a: Use nullable types with defaults or factories to create optional services.
Define interfaces before implementations to guide architecture.
Wrap trait in a small testable class or use anonymous classes in tests.

php_database.title: Databases (PDO & ORMs)
php_database.summary: Use PDO prepared statements, transactions and be aware of N+1s when using ORMs.
php_database.details:
Learn more — practical relational database patterns for PHP apps:

SQL safety & usage
- Always use parameterized queries with PDO or prepared statements from your ORM. Avoid building SQL strings by concatenation.

Transactions & concurrency
- Use transactions for multi-step updates, keep them short, and be aware of isolation levels. Use consistent ordering of resource access to reduce deadlocks.

ORM caution
- Watch for N+1 queries; use eager loading or explicit joins and profile with query log or DB slow query log.

Migrations & ops
- Use migration tools (Phinx, Doctrine Migrations) and include rollback paths for risky changes. Run migrations in CI/staging before production and ensure backups are available.

Scaling
- Use read replicas for scaling reads and caching layers (Redis, memcached) to reduce DB load for common queries.

examples.code:
<?php
$pdo = new PDO('mysql:host=127.0.0.1;dbname=test', 'user', 'pass');
$stmt = $pdo->prepare('SELECT name FROM users WHERE id = :id');
$stmt->execute(['id' => 42]);
$user = $stmt->fetch(PDO::FETCH_ASSOC);

interview.a: Always use prepared statements and bound parameters.
PHP Data Objects — a database abstraction layer supporting prepared statements.
Object-Relational Mapper mapping DB rows to objects (e.g., Doctrine, Eloquent).
Excessive queries due to lazy-loading related records in loops.
Begin a transaction, commit on success and rollback on errors.
Reusing DB connections to reduce overhead (managed by drivers/infra).

interview.q: How to index DB tables properly?
interview.a: Create indexes on frequently searched/joined columns, but avoid over-indexing.
Use migration tools to version schema changes and apply them reliably.
Keep DB credentials in environment variables or secret stores, not in VCS.

interview.q: When to use prepared statements vs ORM?
interview.a: Use prepared statements for raw SQL performance-sensitive queries; ORM for productivity.

interview.q: How to paginate large result sets?
interview.a: Use cursor-based pagination or limit/offset with care for performance.
Measure queries to find slow statements and optimize or add indexes.
Partition data across instances based on keys, handle routing in the app.
Access tables in consistent order and keep transactions short.
Cache heavyweight queries in Redis or memcached with TTLs.

interview.q: How to handle migrations in CI/CD?
interview.a: Run migrations as part of deploy process with rollbacks and backups.

interview.q: How to store JSON in DB?
interview.a: Use JSON column types (if available) and index JSON paths as needed.
Use transactional tests, in-memory DBs, or containerized ephemeral DBs.
A model where data becomes consistent over time across distributed stores.
For read-heavy systems to improve performance, at the cost of complexity.

php_rest.title: REST APIs & JSON
php_rest.summary: Design endpoints, use proper status codes, validation and documentation.
php_rest.details:
Learn more — API design, error handling and evolving contracts:

Design & semantics
- Use nouns in URIs, HTTP verbs for actions, and proper status codes: 200, 201 (create), 204 (no content), 400/422 (validation), 401 (auth), 403 (authorization), 404 (not found), 429 (rate limit), 500 (server error).

Validation & errors
- Validate inputs server-side and return structured error payloads containing code, message and optionally field-level details. Use consistent schemas and document them in OpenAPI.

Versioning & compatibility
- Version early (URL or header) and follow a deprecation strategy. Keep changes backward-compatible when possible; if breaking changes are needed, bump major version.

Security & performance
- Use TLS, authenticate with OAuth2/JWT where applicable, throttle abusive clients and set cache headers (ETag, Cache-Control). Consider pagination and filtering for large lists.

examples.code:
<?php
header('Content-Type: application/json'); http_response_code(200);
echo json_encode(['data' => ['id'=>1, 'name'=>'Alice']]);

interview.a: Statelessness, resource-based URIs, standard HTTP verbs and proper status codes.
201 Created, usually with a Location header.
Validate inputs server-side and return clear error payloads with 4xx codes.
Use URL versioning (/v1/) or header-based versioning and maintain backward compatibility.
Making repeated requests have the same effect as a single request.
Provide limit/offset or cursor-based pagination and include metadata.
Use OpenAPI/Swagger to describe endpoints, schemas and examples.
Use tokens (JWT/OAuth) or session-based auth depending on clients.
Set Content-Type and Accept headers for content negotiation.
Return structured error objects with codes and messages.
Hypermedia links in responses to navigate the API; less common in many APIs.
Use HTTPS, rate-limiting, authentication and input validation.
GraphQL fits flexible queries but adds complexity; REST is simpler for standard CRUD.
Use chunked transfer encoding or pagination to avoid large payloads.
Respect Accept header and return the requested format when possible.
Use integration tests and contract tests to validate behavior.
Implement quotas per client and return 429 with Retry-After header.

interview.q: What is CORS and why care?
interview.a: Cross-Origin Resource Sharing controls browser access to resources; configure appropriately.
Use nouns, hierarchical paths and avoid verbs in URIs.

interview.q: What are cache headers for APIs?
interview.a: Use Cache-Control and ETag/Last-Modified to enable client/proxy caching.

php_security.title: Security & Best Practices
php_security.summary: Sanitize input, use prepared statements, CSRF protections and secure file handling.
php_security.details:
Learn more — security checklist and hardening steps:

Input & output
- Validate and sanitize inputs with whitelists; escape output according to context: HTML, JS, URL, CSS.

Authentication & sessions
- Use password_hash/password_verify for passwords. Use secure session storage, regenerate session IDs on login and use HttpOnly/Secure/SameSite cookie flags.

Network & infra
- Enforce HTTPS with HSTS; terminate TLS at load balancer and validate headers. Apply rate limits, WAF rules and IP-level protections where necessary.

Dependencies & supply chain
- Use composer audit, pin versions, scan images for vulnerable CVEs, and consider SBOMs for higher security assurance.

Incident readiness
- Centralize logs, implement anomaly alerts, have response runbooks and rotate credentials periodically.

examples.code:
<?php
$hash = password_hash('s3cr3t', PASSWORD_DEFAULT);
if(password_verify($input, $hash)){ /* ok */ }

interview.a: Use prepared statements and parameterized queries.
Escape output with htmlspecialchars and use Content Security Policy.
Use CSRF tokens and same-site cookies or framework CSRF protections.
Use password_hash and password_verify with strong algorithms.
Validate file types, store outside web root, and scan for malware.
Use environment variables or secret managers, not VCS.

interview.q: What is input validation vs output escaping?
interview.a: Validate inputs for correctness; escape outputs for the target context.
Redirect all traffic to HTTPS and use HSTS headers.
Regenerate session ID at login and use secure cookies.
Use HttpOnly, Secure, and SameSite attributes.

interview.q: How to prevent remote code execution?
interview.a: Avoid eval(), validate inputs and run minimal permission scripts.
Scan for vulnerabilities and update dependencies regularly.
Avoid logging secrets and use structured logs with access controls.

interview.q: What is rate limiting for security?
interview.a: Throttle requests to prevent brute-force attacks.
Use role/permission checks and enforce on server side.
Avoid leaking internal details; provide user-friendly messages.
A header-based mechanism to restrict what resources can be loaded/executed.

interview.q: How to secure cookies across domains?
interview.a: Use proper domain and sameSite settings; prefer server-side sessions.

interview.q: When to use prepared statements vs escaping?
interview.a: Prepared statements are preferred for DB queries; escaping for output contexts.
Multiple overlapping security measures to reduce risk.

php_sessions.title: Sessions, Cookies & State
php_sessions.summary: Server-side sessions, secure cookies and stateless alternatives with care.
php_sessions.details:
Learn more — managing state robustly across environments:

Session stores
- Use Redis or DB-backed session handlers for multi-instance deployments. Avoid file-based sessions in distributed apps.

Security & lifecycle
- Regenerate session IDs after privilege change, enforce timeouts and implement absolute expiry. Clear session on logout and invalidate tokens on password change.

Stateless alternatives
- JWTs can be used for stateless authentication but plan for revocation (short TTLs + refresh tokens or server-side blacklist).

Performance
- Keep session payloads small by storing identifiers; fetch full state from DB/cache as needed. Monitor session store memory and purge expired entries.

examples.code:
<?php
session_start(); $_SESSION['user_id'] = 123;

interview.a: Call session_start() before output to initialize session data.
Use secure, HttpOnly and SameSite cookies and store session data server-side.
To prevent session fixation, regenerate after privilege changes like login.

interview.q: When to use JWT vs sessions?
interview.a: JWTs fit stateless APIs; sessions are simpler for server-side stateful auth.

interview.q: How to store sessions in distributed systems?
interview.a: Use centralized stores like Redis or database-backed session handlers.
Attributes like Secure, HttpOnly and SameSite to control cookie behavior.
Set proper session.gc_maxlifetime and implement inactivity timeouts.
Destroy session data and clear session cookie on logout.
Use TLS, rotate IDs, and tie sessions to user IP/agent heuristics.

interview.q: How to share sessions across subdomains?
interview.a: Set cookie domain to parent domain, with secure flags.

interview.q: How to store minimal data in sessions?
interview.a: Store identifiers; retrieve full data from DB to keep sessions small.
Mechanism preventing concurrent writes to the same session to avoid races.

interview.q: How to use cookies safely for sensitive data?
interview.a: Never store secrets directly in cookies; use references and server-side stores.

interview.q: How to handle cookies for APIs?
interview.a: Prefer token-based auth for cross-origin APIs or configure CORS and credentials.

interview.q: What is SameSite Lax vs Strict?
interview.a: Lax allows some cross-site navigations; Strict blocks most cross-site requests.

interview.q: How to manage session GC in high-traffic apps?
interview.a: Use external session stores and tune TTLs rather than relying on GC.
Use integration tests that simulate user flows and session persistence.

interview.q: How to recover from corrupted sessions?
interview.a: Rotate ID, clear data safely, and log incidents for investigation.

interview.q: How to encrypt session data at rest?
interview.a: Encrypt storage or use infrastructure that provides encryption for session stores.
Log session creation, changes and logout events for security monitoring.

php_performance.title: Performance & Caching
php_performance.summary: OPcache, Redis/memcached, optimize hot paths and profile before optimizing.
php_performance.details:
Learn more — profiling, caching and scaling strategies:

Profile-driven optimization
- Start with profiling (Xdebug, Blackfire, Tideways) to find hotspots and high-allocation paths before optimizing.

Caching layers
- Use OPcache for code, HTTP cache headers for clients/CDNs, and Redis/memcached for application-level caches. Implement cache invalidation strategies carefully (cache-aside, write-through).

DB & I/O
- Optimize queries, add appropriate indexes, use pagination/cursors and offload heavy reports to dedicated batch jobs or read replicas.

Infrastructure
- Keep app servers stateless, scale horizontally behind a load balancer and manage shared state in centralized stores. Use autoscaling and circuit breakers for dependent services.

examples.code:
<?php
// naive cache pattern
$key = 'user:'.$id; $cached = $redis->get($key);
if(!$cached){ $cached = fetchFromDb($id); $redis->set($key, json_encode($cached), 3600); }

interview.a: A bytecode cache that stores compiled PHP for faster execution.
Use Xdebug for dev and Blackfire/Tideways for production profiling.
Cache expensive computations or DB queries with predictable TTLs.

interview.q: What is cold start vs warm start?
interview.a: Cold start loads resources anew; warm start benefits from caches and ready processes.
Free large variables, use generators and avoid loading entire datasets.
Yield-based iterators that allow lazy sequences to save memory.
Add indexes, avoid N+1, and select only needed columns.

interview.q: When to use Redis vs memcached?
interview.a: Redis for advanced data structures and persistence; memcached for simple cache.
Measure request timings end-to-end and for internal services.
Use load testing tools in non-production environments with realistic data.

interview.q: What is code hot path optimization?
interview.a: Focus on optimizing frequently executed code for best ROI.
Adjust pm settings, process counts and memory limits to match workload.

interview.q: How to use CDN with PHP apps?
interview.a: Offload static assets and aggressive caching to reduce server load.
Use Cache-Control, ETag, and Vary headers appropriately.
Profile first, identify bottlenecks, then optimize targeted areas.
Batch disk/db writes and use in-memory caching where appropriate.
Using libs like Swoole or ReactPHP to run non-blocking code.
Stateless app servers behind load balancers with shared caches and session stores.

interview.q: How to handle large file uploads efficiently?
interview.a: Stream uploads to storage rather than loading in memory.

interview.q: How to monitor performance in production?
interview.a: Use APM tools, metrics, and health checks to track performance trends.

php_swoole.title: Async / Swoole & Workers
php_swoole.summary: Use Swoole/ReactPHP for async services; manage memory and lifecycle carefully.
php_swoole.details:
Learn more — building and operating async PHP services:

When to use async runtimes
- Use Swoole or ReactPHP for workloads with many concurrent I/O operations (websockets, long polling). For CPU-heavy work, offload to worker processes.

Lifecycle & memory
- Long-running processes require careful memory management: detect and fix leaks, periodically recycle processes via supervisors and monitor RSS.

Compatibility
- Some libraries expect per-request lifecycle; isolate or adapt them for coroutine contexts. Prefer non-blocking clients or delegate blocking calls to thread pools.

Observability
- Instrument coroutines with tracing, log per-request IDs and ensure graceful draining on SIGTERM for rolling upgrades.

examples.code:
<?php
// Swoole pseudo
$server->on('request', function($req, $res){ $res->end('Hello Swoole'); });

interview.a: A PHP extension enabling asynchronous, coroutine-based servers.
For high-concurrency low-latency backends where async model helps.
Lightweight primitives for concurrency introduced in PHP 8.1.
A pure-PHP event-loop library for asynchronous programming.

interview.q: How to manage memory in long-running processes?
interview.a: Monitor memory, avoid leaks, and periodically restart or recycle workers.
Use non-blocking clients or delegate blocking tasks to dedicated workers.
A function that can pause and resume, enabling cooperative multitasking.
Run as a service with supervisors and expose via TCP/HTTP endpoints.
Use event-loop aware testing or integration tests in controlled environments.

interview.q: How to share state between worker processes?
interview.a: Use external stores like Redis rather than shared memory.

interview.q: What are common pitfalls with async PHP?
interview.a: Library compatibility, unexpected blocking calls, and lifecycle management.

interview.q: How to handle signals in long-running processes?
interview.a: Register handlers to gracefully shutdown and cleanup resources.

interview.q: How to integrate async with frameworks?
interview.a: Use adapters or isolate async components to avoid changing sync codebase.
Mechanism to slow producers when consumers can't keep up in async systems.

interview.q: How to perform CPU-bound work in async apps?
interview.a: Offload to worker pools or separate services to avoid blocking event loop.
Use specialized logs, traces and sampling profilers aware of coroutines.
Set per-operation timeouts and handle cancellations in async flows.
Run multiple worker processes per machine and load balance across machines.

interview.q: How to manage dependencies in async apps?
interview.a: Ensure libraries are non-blocking or isolated behind worker boundaries.

interview.q: What are typical use-cases for Swoole?
interview.a: Web sockets, realtime services, microservices and RPC backends.

php_testing.title: Testing & Tooling
php_testing.summary: PHPUnit, static analysis (PHPStan/Psalm), linters and CI integration.
php_testing.details:
Learn more — testing pyramid and practical tooling:

Unit tests
- Keep fast, deterministic unit tests; mock external dependencies and assert behavior.

Integration tests
- Use ephemeral containers for DBs/services in CI to run realistic integration tests. Test migrations and schema changes.

Static analysis & mutation
- Run PHPStan/Psalm in CI and use mutation testing (infection) to evaluate test quality.

Test hygiene
- Track flaky tests, quarantine until stable and parallelize tests where possible to shorten CI times.

examples.code:
<?php
// PHPUnit example
use PHPUnit\Framework\TestCase;
class MathTest extends TestCase{ public function testAdd(){ $this->assertEquals(5, 2+3); } }

interview.q: What testing tools are common in PHP?
interview.a: PHPUnit for unit tests, Pest as a wrapper, and integration with CI tools.

interview.q: What is unit vs integration test?
interview.a: Unit tests isolate components; integration tests verify component interactions.
Use PHPUnit mocks or mocking libraries to replace collaborators in tests.
Ensuring tests do not depend on each other's state or external systems.
Use mocks, stubs, or spin ephemeral services in CI with containers.
End-to-end tests verifying user flows often via browser automation.

interview.q: How to run tests in CI?
interview.a: Install dependencies and run vendor/bin/phpunit as part of pipeline.
A metric showing percentage of code exercised by tests; not a quality guarantee.
Prepare known data states for tests, either via factories or DB seeders.
Use transactional tests or ephemeral DBs to isolate side effects.
Altering code to ensure tests detect changes, used to assess test quality.
Parallelize tests, avoid heavyweight integration for unit suites.

interview.q: What is static analysis role in testing?
interview.a: Catch issues early via tools like PHPStan or Psalm complementing tests.
Introduce seams, extract logic, and add characterization tests gradually.

interview.q: What is flaky test and how to avoid it?
interview.a: Intermittent failing test; avoid by removing timing/dependency on external state.
Use event-loop-aware test helpers or integration tests with controlled async events.
Invoke commands programmatically and assert output and exit codes.
Mirror src/ structure under tests/ for easy mapping and discovery.
Run tests automatically on commits or PRs in CI to catch regressions early.

interview.q: How to measure test flakiness over time?
interview.a: Track flaky failures in CI and quarantine unstable tests until fixed.

php_debug.title: Debugging & Profiling
php_debug.summary: Xdebug, structured logs and production-safe profiling.
php_debug.details:
Learn more — observability, tracing and production-safe diagnostics:

Local debugging
- Use Xdebug for step-through debugging and for collecting profiles. Keep dev config separate from production.

Production observability
- Use structured logs, metrics and traces (OpenTelemetry) to correlate user requests and backend calls. Use sampling for traces to limit overhead.

Remote diagnosis
- Capture request context (headers, user id, trace id) on errors, but redact sensitive fields. Keep error retention and rotation policies in place.

examples.code:
<?php
error_log(json_encode(['level'=>'error','msg'=>'failed','id'=>123]));

interview.a: For step-debugging, stack traces and profiling in development.
It can leak sensitive data and break response formats.
A list of calls showing execution path at an error point.
Use structured logs with severity levels and contextual metadata.
Tools that periodically sample stack traces to find hotspots with low overhead.
Profile, analyze hotspots, and optimize critical paths.
Use error tracking services like Sentry to collect exceptions and contexts.

interview.q: How to enable verbose errors in dev only?
interview.a: Show full error pages locally and generic messages in production.
Use tracing systems like OpenTelemetry to follow requests across services.
Track allocations, use profilers and look for static caches or closures holding refs.

interview.q: How to reproduce production bugs locally?
interview.a: Capture inputs, logs and environment details to replicate the conditions.
Logging only when certain conditions or thresholds are met to reduce noise.

interview.q: How to strip debug code in production?
interview.a: Use build steps to remove dev-only hooks and ensure debug flags are off.
Validate assumptions in code during development to catch regressions early.
Attach a debugger to a remote process via network for step-through debugging.
Rate-limit, aggregate, and index logs for efficient search and alerting.

interview.q: How to annotate code for observability?
interview.a: Emit structured metadata and spans to help tracing and debugging.
Record interleavings, use deterministic tests and logging to reproduce.
Protect them with auth and restrict access to internal networks.

interview.q: How to combine logs and metrics for debugging?
interview.a: Use logs for context and metrics to detect anomalies and trends.

php_deployment.title: Deployment & Containers
php_deployment.summary: Dockerize apps, configure PHP-FPM + Nginx, use CI/CD and health checks.
php_deployment.details:
Learn more — release engineering and operational best practices:

Build pipelines
- CI should run tests, static analysis and build artifacts (images or deployable archives). Sign and store artifacts in an immutable registry.

Deploy strategies
- Use blue/green or canary deployments with health checks and observability to limit blast radius. Automate database migrations with care — prefer backward-compatible migrations.

Runtime hardening
- Scan images for vulnerabilities, run processes with least privilege, enable resource limits and centralize logging/metrics for monitoring.
php_deployment.cheat:
// Dockerfile sketch
FROM composer:2 as build
WORKDIR /app
COPY composer.json composer.lock ./
RUN composer install --no-dev --optimize-autoloader
COPY . .
RUN composer dump-autoload -o
FROM php:8.1-fpm
COPY --from=build /app /var/www/html

// deploy flow
build -> test -> push -> run migrations -> health-check -> promote

examples.code:
# Dockerfile (sketch)
FROM php:8.1-fpm AS build
WORKDIR /app
COPY composer.* ./
RUN composer install --no-dev --optimize-autoloader
FROM php:8.1-fpm
COPY --from=build /app /var/www/html

interview.a: Use multi-stage Dockerfiles, install dependencies in build stage, and copy artifacts to runtime image.
FastCGI Process Manager that runs PHP worker processes behind webservers.
Use rolling updates, blue/green deployments, or readiness probes in orchestration.
Docker technique to separate build-time tools from runtime image to reduce size.

interview.q: How to run migrations safely during deploy?
interview.a: Run migrations in a separate step with backups and checks for downtime.
Expose endpoints returning readiness and liveness status for orchestrators.

interview.q: How to manage secrets in containers?
interview.a: Use secret stores or orchestrator-native secrets rather than env vars in images.

interview.q: How to tune PHP-FPM for concurrency?
interview.a: Adjust pm.max_children and related settings to match memory and load.
Pin base images, install deterministic dependencies and use composer.lock.

interview.q: How to run scheduled tasks in containers?
interview.a: Use cron in sidecar containers or external schedulers invoking jobs.

interview.q: How to handle logs in containers?
interview.a: Write logs to stdout/stderr and collect via centralized logging.
Use slim/base images and clean package caches during build.
Route a fraction of traffic to new version and monitor metrics before full rollout.
Keep infrastructure to route traffic between blue and green environments.
Replace instances rather than mutate them to reduce configuration drift.

interview.q: How to handle database migrations in zero-downtime?
interview.a: Use backward-compatible migrations and phased deploys.

interview.q: How to update dependencies in production?
interview.a: Test in staging, pin versions, and perform canary rollouts.

interview.q: What is health check vs readiness?
interview.a: Liveness ensures process running; readiness indicates service ready to serve traffic.

interview.q: How to rollback a failed deploy?
interview.a: Use orchestrator rollbacks or switch traffic back to previous stable version.
Scan images for vulnerabilities and use minimal base images.

php_best_practices.title: Best Practices & PSRs
php_best_practices.summary: Follow PSRs, use strict types, avoid global state, and enforce tests and static analysis in CI.
php_best_practices.details:
Learn more — team-level practices and long-term maintainability:

Quality & process
- Enforce code style, static analysis and tests in CI. Use code reviews focused on design, test coverage and security.

API stewardship
- Keep public APIs small, document changes, publish migration guides and follow semantic versioning for libraries.

Architecture
- Design for observability: emit metrics, structured logs and traces. Keep components decoupled and well-bounded.

Continuous improvement
- Track technical debt, schedule refactors, and use performance budgets to prevent regressing on latency and memory footprints.

examples.code:
<?php
// Service with DI
class UserService{ private UserRepo $repo; public function __construct(UserRepo $r){ $this->repo = $r; } }

interview.q: What are PSRs and why follow them?
interview.a: Standards improving interoperability and consistency across projects.
Catch type and logical errors early before runtime.
Automated testing and builds triggered on commits/PRs.
Use DI, avoid singletons and pass dependencies explicitly.
Easier to test, reason about and reuse.
Standardize levels, formats and correlation IDs across services.
To avoid breaking downstream consumers and to enable safe upgrades.
Follow semver to communicate breaking changes and compatible updates.
Gradually roll out features and quickly disable if issues arise.
Keep them deterministic, small, and fast with clear assertions.

interview.q: What is code review best practice?
interview.a: Review for clarity, tests, security, performance and design.
Track, prioritize and allocate time to refactor and pay down debt.

interview.q: How to document code and APIs?
interview.a: Provide inline docs, README guides and OpenAPI for public endpoints.
Use linters and formatters like PHP_CodeSniffer or PHPCS with CI checks.
To allow deploys without downtime and avoid incompatible schema changes.
Emit metrics, structured logs and traces for monitoring and debugging.
Refactor when adding features, fixing bugs or when code readability suffers.
Audit, pin versions and sandbox when possible.
Design interfaces and contracts first, then implement to the spec.
Enable secure settings by default and require opt-in to relax security.
